response_text:
# Memory Management in Rust

Rust uses a unique approach that provides memory safety **without a garbage collector**, catching errors at compile time instead of runtime.

## Core Concepts

### 1. Ownership
Every value has exactly one owner, and the value is dropped when the owner goes out of scope.

```rust
fn main() {
    let s = String::from("hello");  // s owns the string
    println!("{}", s);
} // s goes out of scope, memory is freed automatically
```

### 2. Move Semantics
Ownership can be transferred (moved) to another variable.

```rust
let s1 = String::from("hello");
let s2 = s1;  // ownership moves to s2

// println!("{}", s1);  // ERROR: s1 is no longer valid
println!("{}", s2);     // OK
```

**Note:** Simple types like integers implement `Copy`, so they're duplicated instead of moved.

```rust
let x = 5;
let y = x;  // x is copied, not moved
println!("{} {}", x, y);  // Both valid
```

### 3. Borrowing & References
You can lend access to a value without transferring ownership.

```rust
fn print_length(s: &String) {  // borrows s, doesn't own it
    println!("Length: {}", s.len());
}

let s = String::from("hello");
print_length(&s);       // pass a reference
println!("{}", s);      // s still valid here
```

### Borrowing Rules (enforced at compile time)
| Rule | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Multiple immutable borrows | ✅ Allowed |
| One mutable borrow | ✅ Allowed |
| Mutable + any other borrow | ❌ Not allowed |

```rust
let mut s = String::from("hello");

let r1 = &s;      // OK
let r2 = &s;      // OK - multiple immutable borrows
// let r3 = &mut s;  // ERROR - can't mix mutable and immutable

println!("{} {}", r1,
